Mangalagiri- Narasimha Kshetra

# Mangalagiri- Narasimha Kshetra

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 03, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Mangalagiri is such a fascinating blend of sacred tradition and living craft. The Panakala Swamy temple, where devotees offer jaggery water to Lord Narasimha, is unique among Narasimha Kshetras, its ritual emphasizes simplicity and devotion rather than grandeur. That spiritual aura is beautifully complemented by the town’s handloom heritage, Mangalagiri cottons and sarees, with their distinctive Nizam border and fine weave, are prized across India for their elegance and durability.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ReD2!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc137d47e-c1e9-4c9f-b496-a47aa285b204_320x240.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ReD2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc137d47e-c1e9-4c9f-b496-a47aa285b204_320x240.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6Azm!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe980f96f-5554-4b7b-b797-6ec11e4ec047_250x187.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6Azm!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe980f96f-5554-4b7b-b797-6ec11e4ec047_250x187.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!umqi!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fafa4117f-2699-4c54-821d-0a0d65182e6a_360x240.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!umqi!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fafa4117f-2699-4c54-821d-0a0d65182e6a_360x240.jpeg)

It’s very name, “Auspicious Hill,” tied to Lakshmi Devi’s meditation, makes the landscape itself feel sanctified. The older name *Thotadri* connects it to antiquity, and its recognition as one of the eight Mahakshetrams of Vishnu places it in the same exalted company as Srirangam and Venkatadri. The trio of Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy at the foot, Panakala Swamy midway, and Gandala Narasimha Swamy at the summit, creates a natural pilgrimage arc.

Panakala Swamy is the heart of Mangalagiri’s spiritual identity. The image of Narasimha with his mouth eternally open is striking, almost symbolic of divine receptivity. The daily ritual of pouring *panakam* (jaggery water) into that open mouth is unlike any other temple practice, it’s both intimate and communal, as devotees feel they are directly nourishing the deity.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KfMe!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5f08ec0c-0085-4228-a181-891abd3a6e45_217x233.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KfMe!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5f08ec0c-0085-4228-a181-891abd3a6e45_217x233.jpeg)

As per the Sthala purana, Vishnu appeared in the form of Narasimha to kill an Asura Namuchi, who had a boon that he could not be killed by anything wet or dry. Vishnu, dipped his Sudarshan Chakra in sea foam, and destroyed the Asura with fire exhaling from him. However the Devatas could not face the fierce form of Narasimha, and he was finally appeased with Amrit. He then declared that he would be appeased with Ghee in Tretayugam, Milk in Dwaparayugam and Panakam in Kali Yugam, which explains the practice here.

The very unique aspect is that whatever the amount of Panakam that is fed into the opening through a conch, he only consumes a part of it, and throws out the remaining part. And you can actually hear the gargling sound, as if he is drinking the Panakam. Also inspite of the large amount of Panakam fed to Swamy, you won’t find a single ant in the vicinity. It’s believed that this hill was once a volcano, and the gurgling sound you hear, is due to the sulphur deposits, present in it.

The Panakala Swamy temple really stands apart because of its absence of a traditional idol, instead, just the large opening with the metal face of Narasimha placed around it. That starkness makes the ritual of pouring *panakam* even more dramatic, as if the hill itself is the deity. The gurgling sound during the offering, the belief that the hill was once volcanic, and the natural cave tunnel said to connect all the way to the Undavalli caves. The temple can be reached either by steps or a ghat road going all the way up.

Right at the start of the steps, pilgrims are greeted not only by faith but by memory of empire, the **edict installed by** Timmarasu **in 1515**, after Sri Krishnadeva Raya’s capture of Kondavidu fort. It’s a reminder that Mangalagiri is not just a spiritual site but also a witness to political triumphs. The **Jaya Stambha**, commemorating that victory, stands as a symbol of royal power and divine sanction,almost as if the ascent to Panakala Swamy begins with a declaration of worldly conquest before moving into the realm of spiritual surrender.

The Lakshmi Narasimha temple at the foot of Mangalagiri hill is a jewel of antiquity in Andhra Pradesh. Tradition holds that the **main deity was installed by Yudhishtira himself**, which immediately ties the temple to the epic lineage of the Mahabharata. That mythic origin gives the shrine a sense of timelessness, as though it has stood as a witness to both divine and human history.

Architecturally, the temple is renowned for its **intricate sculptures and carvings**, pillars adorned with mythological motifs, mandapas that echo with the artistry of Vijayanagara craftsmanship, and a sanctum that radiates quiet majesty. It’s not just a place of worship but a living museum of sacred art, where stone itself seems to breathe devotion.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-rKC!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0813126a-87fc-4e5e-9506-026ae8617837_512x339.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-rKC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0813126a-87fc-4e5e-9506-026ae8617837_512x339.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The massive Gali Gopuram of the Lakshmi Narasimha temple at a height of 153 ft, 59 ft wide and 11 storeys tall, was constructed by Vasireddy Venkatadri Naidu, the local chieftain of Amaravati.He brought in master *sthapathis* from **Kanchipuram**, ensuring that the craftsmanship carried the finesse of Tamil temple architecture while blending with Andhra traditions.

The sheer scale of the gopuram means it dominates the landscape,you can see it from miles away, rising like a sentinel of faith. Its towering presence serves as both a spiritual beacon and a historical marker, reminding pilgrims that this temple is not just a place of worship but a testament to regional artistry and patronage.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VDIZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd59780a2-1de9-4f0e-9683-28cb9dcf7ea2_244x240.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VDIZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd59780a2-1de9-4f0e-9683-28cb9dcf7ea2_244x240.jpeg)

The Lakshmi Narasimha temple at the foot of Mangalagiri hill is layered with both **mythic consecration** and **historic patronage**. While tradition ties the installation of the deity to Yudhishtira, the temple’s **rich sculptures** were later commissioned by **Timma Rajaiah**, the Kondavidu chieftain. His contribution gave the shrine its artistic depth,pillars and mandapas alive with carvings that narrate divine stories in stone.

Adding to this sacred landscape is the **Kalyani Pushkarini**, the large temple tank at the center of Mangalagiri. Built by the **Vijayanagar rulers in 1558**, it anchors the town both spiritually and socially. Pushkarinis were not just ritual reservoirs but also community lifelines, symbolizing purity and renewal. Here, the waters reflect the towering gopuram and the hill beyond, tying together devotion, architecture, and nature.

The temple has main deity of Shri Narasimha with Lakshmi made of stone. The Dakshanavrutha Sankham blown in the temple was gifted by the Maratha ruler of Thanjavur, Serfoji, such conches are considered extremely rare and auspicious, symbolizing divine resonance. He also gifted a garland made of 108 saligramas, each sacred stone representing Vishnu himself, strung together into a circle of cosmic presence.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!EjXj!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F322fc652-ea69-438a-8f2b-1a9d504dcb45_442x240.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!EjXj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F322fc652-ea69-438a-8f2b-1a9d504dcb45_442x240.jpeg)

The **Ganda Narasimha temple** at the summit of Mangalagiri is striking precisely because of its simplicity. Unlike the grand sanctums below, here there is **no deity in form**, just a small room with a **deepam (lamp)**, lit by devotees with ghee made from cow’s milk. This act of lighting the lamp is believed to bring relief from misery, a quiet offering of light against darkness.

As per historical records, Mangalagiri was part of the Satavahana kingdom, when they ruled from Dharanikota, near Amaravati around 225 BC, later it passed into the rule of Pallavas. Between 420-620 AD it was the capital of the Vishnukundinas. It was later part of the Eastern Chalukya kingdom from 630 AD, before being captured by the Kakatiyas in 1182, and was attacked by the Delhi Sultanate, before the Reddy Kings of Kondavidu took over it. It subsequently passed under the control of the Gajapatis, and later Sri Krishna Deva Raya in 1515. With the fall of Hampi post Tallikota, it came under the rule of Golkonda Qutub Shahis and the Nizam later, who in turn ceded it to the British.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!86Nd!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0644792d-834d-47d8-96f9-ce162aeedd6d_259x194.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!86Nd!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0644792d-834d-47d8-96f9-ce162aeedd6d_259x194.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yAGu!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F78b3adec-ab2d-430f-b2f6-e385fcc0203d_358x141.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yAGu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F78b3adec-ab2d-430f-b2f6-e385fcc0203d_358x141.jpeg)

Mangalagiri’s fame extends beyond its temples into the world of **handloom weaving**, especially its **cotton sarees**. These sarees are celebrated for their **fine quality, durability, and distinctive style**. They generally feature plain body with a striking Nizam border, often in contrasting colors.The fabric is woven so tightly that it doesn’t require starching, giving it a natural crispness.
